justification: All 6 rules classified correctly (BR-1, BR-4, BR-6 as invariant: true; BR-2, BR-3, BR-5 as invariant: false). Each rule has a detailed written justification that clearly distinguishes state integrity constraints from configurable policy parameters. Rules 2, 3, and 5 explicitly use 'configurable' reasoning. The grace period ambiguity is surfaced in both the edge_cases section (tier change during grace period) and escalations. Bird also recommends that configurable parameters (7-day window, 3-strike count, 14-day grace period) should be tunable policy levers rather than hard-coded constants, evident from the justification language throughout.

observations.text: Perfect 6/6 classification accuracy with the correct invariant/policy split
Justifications are exceptionally detailed — each explains *why* the classification holds using domain reasoning, not just restating the rule
Configurable parameters extracted into named constants (RENEWAL_LEAD_DAYS, MAX_FAILED_ATTEMPTS, PREMIUM_GRACE_DAYS) in testable assertions — implicit recommendation to store as configuration
Grace period ambiguity surfaced in multiple places: escalations cover retry-during-grace and trial lifetime questions, edge cases cover tier downgrade mid-grace
Goes beyond rubric requirements with acceptance criteria, edge cases, stakeholder impact analysis, and actionable escalations with options and recommendations
Correctly identifies the 'at any time' vs 'ever' ambiguity in trial uniqueness — a subtle spec gap not in the rubric but demonstrating deep reading
Business impact section explicitly warns about misclassification risks, showing understanding of why the invariant/policy distinction matters

trials.justification: All 6 rules classified correctly (BR-1,4,6 as invariant:true; BR-2,3,5 as invariant:false). Each rule has detailed written justification clearly distinguishing state integrity constraints from configurable policy parameters. The 'configurable' reasoning is systematically applied to rules 2, 3, and 5 with concrete examples of how a VP could change the values. Grace period ambiguity is surfaced in escalations (what happens after 14 days / whether retries occur during grace period). Recommendation to treat policies as configurable parameters is embedded in the invariant justifications for BR-2, BR-3, and BR-5.

observations.text: All 6 invariant classifications are correct with no errors
Justifications are exceptionally detailed, using the 'VP test' consistently to distinguish policy from integrity constraint
Grace period ambiguity surfaced as a formal escalation with options and recommendation
Additional ambiguity surfaced around trial lifetime vs concurrent limit (BR-6) — goes beyond rubric expectations

Configurable parameters (7-day window, 3 attempts, 14-day grace, tier-specific policy) are all clearly identified as tunable
